title: When the Walls Are Breached
subtitle: Detection & incident response

hook.metaphor: Even the best-defended castle plans for the day the walls are breached — where the alarm bells hang, where everyone runs, who leads the counter-attack, how you put out the fires and rebuild. In cyber, *assuming you'll eventually be breached* isn't pessimism; it's professionalism. The question isn't only 'will they get in?' but 'how fast will we notice and respond?'
hook.scenario: Attackers were inside a company's network for 200 days before anyone noticed — quietly stealing data the whole time. When they were finally spotted, there was no plan, so the response was chaos. Two failures here, not one. What were they?

learn: First failure: **detection**. The scariest breaches are the ones you don't see — our attackers had 200 days (the industry calls this 'dwell time'). Good detection means **monitoring and logging**, often via a **SOC (Security Operations Centre)** watching for anomalies, so you spot intruders in hours, not months.

Second failure: **no incident response plan**. A standard playbook has clear phases — **detect, contain, eradicate, recover, and learn**. *Contain* (stop the bleeding — isolate affected systems), *eradicate* (remove the attacker), *recover* (restore from clean backups), and *learn* (a blameless post-mortem so it doesn't recur). Deciding all this *during* a crisis guarantees chaos.

A breach also triggers **legal clocks**. Privacy laws require notifying regulators (and often affected people) fast — Australia's **Notifiable Data Breaches** scheme, the EU's **72-hour** GDPR rule, India's DPDP breach duties. Knowing the clock is part of the plan, not an afterthought.

Underrated truth: **backups are your ultimate insurance**, especially against ransomware. Clean, tested, offline backups mean you can recover without paying. A backup you've never tested restoring is just a hope — practise the recovery before you need it.
And rehearse: tabletop **exercises** (a simulated breach drill) turn a panicked scramble into a calm, practised routine — exactly the resilience mindset from Operational Risk's fire-drill.
bridge: So at work, you can assess breach-readiness with two questions beyond 'are we secure?': *How fast would we even notice?* (detection/monitoring) and *Do we have a tested plan and clean backups for when it happens?* (response/recovery). Assume breach, shrink dwell time, and rehearse the response — because the difference between an incident and a catastrophe is usually how fast and how calmly you react.

interactions.title: Order the response
interactions.prompt: Sort each action into the incident-response phase it belongs to.

items.label: Isolate the infected machines from the network
Remove the attacker's access and malware
Run a blameless post-mortem to prevent recurrence
Cut off the compromised account immediately
Bring services back online and verify integrity

interactions.title: 200 days unseen
interactions.prompt: Attackers lurked for 200 days, then the response was chaos. The two core fixes?

options.label: Just buy a bigger firewall to keep them out.
options.feedback: Prevention alone failed — and will sometimes fail for everyone. The gaps here were *detection* (200 days unseen) and *response* (no plan), neither of which a firewall fixes.

options.label: Add monitoring/detection to shrink dwell time, and build a tested response plan.
options.feedback: Exactly. You assume breach: detect fast (monitoring/SOC) and respond calmly (a rehearsed detect-contain-eradicate-recover-learn plan with clean backups).

options.label: Pay the attackers to go away.
options.feedback: Paying funds crime, doesn't guarantee recovery, and ignores the real gaps. With tested backups and a plan, you recover on your own terms.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Assume you'll be breached — so invest in detecting it fast and in a rehearsed plan (contain, eradicate, recover, learn) with clean tested backups, because how fast and calmly you respond decides whether it's an incident or a catastrophe.”

checkpoint.q: Why does long attacker 'dwell time' (e.g. 200 days unseen) matter so much?
options: The longer they're undetected, the more damage and data theft occurs
checkpoint.explain: Undetected attackers keep stealing and spreading — fast detection (monitoring/SOC) shrinks the harm.

checkpoint.q: The phase that immediately stops the bleeding by isolating affected systems is…
checkpoint.explain: Containment isolates the problem first; eradication and recovery come after.

checkpoint.q: Clean, tested, offline backups are especially powerful against…
options: Ransomware — you can recover without paying
checkpoint.explain: If you can restore from clean backups, ransomware loses its leverage — but only if you've tested the restore.
